subject: reading
title: Grade 3 Reading — EOG Practice Test 2
instructions: Read each selection carefully. Then answer the questions. Choose the one best answer for each question unless you are told to select more than one.

passages.title: The Traveler's Soup
passages.text:
Part 1

A tired traveler walked into a small village just as the sun was setting. He had not eaten all day, and his stomach growled like a hungry bear. He knocked on the very first door he saw.

"Could you spare a little food?" he asked the woman who answered.

"We have nothing to share," she said quickly, though the smell of fresh bread drifted out the door behind her. The traveler only smiled politely and walked on. At every house he heard the same answer. The villagers had plenty of food, but they did not want to give any away.

The traveler did not become angry. Instead, he sat down in the middle of the village square, took a smooth gray stone from his pocket, and dropped it into a big iron pot. Then he poured in water and built a small fire beneath it.

A curious boy wandered over. "What are you cooking?" he asked.

"Stone soup," the traveler answered, stirring slowly. "It is the most delicious soup in all the world. This batch is nearly perfect. It only needs a few carrots to make it even better."

Part 2

The boy ran home and returned with three carrots, which the traveler dropped into the pot. Soon a woman appeared. "Stone soup?" she said. "I have never heard of such a thing."

"It is wonderful," the traveler replied. "Though a bit of cabbage would make it richer." The woman hurried off and came back with a head of cabbage.

One by one, the villagers came to see the magic soup. Each time, the traveler mentioned one more thing the soup needed — a handful of beans, a few potatoes, a pinch of salt. Each time, a villager rushed to bring it. Before long, the pot was full and bubbling, and a delicious smell filled the whole square.

When the soup was ready, the traveler shared it with everyone. The villagers ate together, laughing and talking late into the night. They all agreed it was the finest soup they had ever tasted.

"Such a magic stone!" they said.

The traveler only smiled. He knew the soup was not made by the stone at all. It was made by everyone sharing a little of what they had.

passages.title: Marble Enters the Race
passages.text:
Part 1

Marble the snail lived at the bottom of the garden, beneath a broad green leaf. Every spring the garden creatures held a great race from the old fence to the birdbath. And every spring, Marble watched from the sidelines, because everyone knew a snail was far too slow to enter.

"Why bother?" laughed Dash the grasshopper. "You would still be crawling when the leaves turn brown!"

This year, though, Marble decided things would be different. "I am going to race," she announced. The other creatures giggled, but Marble paid them no mind. On the morning of the race, she lined up beside the rabbits, the beetles, and the busy ants.

"Ready, set, go!" called the wise old toad. In a flash, the others sped away. Marble began to glide forward, slow and steady, leaving a shiny silver trail behind her.

Part 2

The day grew hot. By noon, Dash the grasshopper had stopped to nap in the cool shade. The rabbits had wandered off to nibble clover and forgotten the race entirely. The beetles argued about which way to go and ended up walking in circles.

Marble did not stop. She did not rush, and she did not quit. Inch by inch, she crept along the path, past the sleeping grasshopper, past the lost beetles, always moving toward the birdbath.

When the sun began to set, the garden creatures gathered at the finish line, wondering who had won. Then they saw a faint silver trail shining in the evening light. At the very end of that trail sat Marble, resting quietly beside the birdbath.

"Marble won!" cried the ants in surprise.

Marble smiled. "I may be slow," she said, "but I never stopped trying." From that day on, no one in the garden ever laughed at a snail again.

passages.title: Why the Ocean Is Salty
passages.text:
Part 1

Have you ever swallowed a mouthful of ocean water by accident? If so, you know it tastes very salty. But have you ever wondered where all that salt comes from? The answer begins far away from the sea, high up on the land.

Rain falls on rocks and soil all over the land. As the rainwater flows downhill, it picks up tiny bits of salt and other minerals from the rocks. These bits are so small that you cannot see or taste them in a single drop. The water carries them into little streams, and the streams carry them into rivers. Slowly, the rivers carry the salt all the way to the ocean.

Part 2

Rivers add a little salt to the ocean every single day. This has been happening for millions of years. You might think the rivers would carry the salt back out again, but they do not. Here is why.

The sun warms the top of the ocean. Some of the water turns into a gas called water vapor and rises into the air. This change is called evaporation. When water evaporates, the salt is left behind. The fresh water rises into the sky, forms clouds, and falls again as rain. But the salt stays in the ocean.

Because new salt keeps arriving and almost none leaves, the ocean has slowly grown saltier and saltier over time. A few lakes that have no rivers flowing out of them, such as the Great Salt Lake, are even saltier than the ocean. Scientists study ocean water to learn about Earth's past. The salt in the sea is like a record of millions of years of rain, rivers, and sunshine working together.

passages.title: Giants of the Forest
passages.text:
Part 1

Deep in the forests of California grow some of the largest living things on Earth. They are the giant sequoia trees. A single giant sequoia can grow taller than a 25-story building and wider than a city street. Some of these trees have been alive for more than two thousand years. That means a few giant sequoias were already growing before many of the world's oldest cities were even built.

What makes these trees so special is not only their size but also their strength. A giant sequoia has thick, spongy bark that can be almost two feet deep. This bark is like the tree's armor. It protects the tree from insects and even from fire.

Part 2

You might expect fire to be a giant sequoia's worst enemy. Surprisingly, the opposite is true. Forest fires actually help these giants survive. When a fire sweeps through the forest, the heat dries out the sequoia's cones and causes them to pop open. The tiny seeds inside then fall to the ground.

Fire also clears away smaller plants and dead leaves from the forest floor. This makes room for the seeds and lets sunlight reach the soil. The ashes left behind add nutrients that help young trees grow. Without fire, sequoia seeds would have a much harder time sprouting.

For many years, people put out every forest fire they could in order to protect the trees. Later, scientists discovered this was a mistake. Without fire, fewer baby sequoias were growing. Today, forest rangers sometimes set small, careful fires on purpose. These planned fires help the great trees make new seedlings, so the giants of the forest can live on for many more years.

passages.title: Roberto Clemente: A Hero On and Off the Field
passages.text:
Part 1

Roberto Clemente was born in 1934 on the island of Puerto Rico. As a boy, he loved baseball so much that he made his own balls out of old socks and used a stick for a bat. His family did not have much money, but Roberto practiced every chance he got. He dreamed of one day playing in the big leagues.

Roberto's hard work paid off. In 1955, he joined the Pittsburgh Pirates in the United States. At first, life was difficult. He spoke Spanish, while most people around him spoke English. Some people were unkind because he came from a different place. But Roberto kept playing, and he played brilliantly. Fans soon noticed his powerful throws and his amazing hits.

Part 2

Over the years, Roberto became one of the greatest players in baseball history. He won many awards and helped his team win the World Series twice. But Roberto cared about much more than baseball. He believed that people with talent had a duty to help others.

Whenever he could, Roberto used his fame to do good. He visited sick children in hospitals and gave money and food to families in need. He often said that anyone who has the chance to make life better for others, and does not do it, is wasting time on Earth.

In 1972, a terrible earthquake struck the country of Nicaragua. Roberto gathered food and supplies to send to the people there. He even got on a plane to make sure the help arrived safely. Sadly, the plane crashed, and Roberto died. People all over the world were heartbroken. Today, Roberto Clemente is remembered not only as a great athlete, but also as a man who gave his life trying to help others.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, why does the traveler stop in the village?
options: He is hungry and has not eaten all day.
He wants to sell a magic stone.
He is looking for a place to sleep.
He needs to fix his broken pot.
questions.explanation: Part 1 says the traveler had not eaten all day and his stomach growled, so he knocks on doors asking for food.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "spare" as it is used in Part 1?
options: To give a small amount of something away
To cook something over a fire
To buy something at a store
To keep something safe and hidden
questions.explanation: The traveler asks if the woman could "spare a little food," meaning give some of it away.

questions.questionText: Which word best describes the traveler in the story?
options: Selfish
Clever
Lazy
Frightened
questions.explanation: The traveler tricks the villagers into adding food to the pot, which shows he is clever.

questions.questionText: What does the boy bring to add to the soup?
options: A handful of beans
Three carrots
A pinch of salt
A head of cabbage
questions.explanation: In Part 2, the boy runs home and returns with three carrots, which the traveler drops into the pot.

questions.questionText: What is the theme, or lesson, of this story?
options: Special stones can solve any problem.
Hard work is always better than being clever.
It is dangerous to talk to strangers in a village.
When everyone shares a little, much can be made together.
questions.explanation: The last paragraph explains the soup was made by everyone sharing a little of what they had, which is the story's lesson.

questions.questionText: Which detail from the text best supports the idea that the stone itself was not magic?
options: "The villagers had plenty of food, but they did not want to give any away."
"He poured in water and built a small fire beneath it."
"He knew the soup was not made by the stone at all."
"A curious boy wandered over."
questions.explanation: The sentence stating the traveler knew the soup was not made by the stone directly supports that the stone was not magic.

questions.questionText: How are the events in Part 1 and Part 2 connected?
options: Part 2 happens before the events in Part 1.
Part 1 explains a problem that is never solved in Part 2.
Part 1 and Part 2 both describe the same meal eaten on two different days.
Part 1 shows the villagers refusing to share, and Part 2 shows the traveler's plan slowly changing their minds.
questions.explanation: Part 1 sets up the villagers' refusal to share; Part 2 shows the traveler's stone-soup trick gradually getting them to contribute food.

questions.questionText: Select the two foods that villagers added to the soup in Part 2.
options: Apples
Potatoes
Cabbage
Cheese
questions.explanation: Part 2 lists cabbage and potatoes (along with beans and salt) as things villagers brought. Apples and cheese are never mentioned.

questions.questionText: Where does the great race take place?
options: From the birdbath to the broad green leaf
Across a field of clover
From the old fence to the birdbath
Around the old toad's pond
questions.explanation: Part 1 states the race goes from the old fence to the birdbath.

questions.questionText: Why does Marble decide to enter the race this year?
options: Dash the grasshopper dares her to do it.
She is now the fastest creature in the garden.
She decides she wants things to be different and tries.
The wise old toad orders her to race.
questions.explanation: Part 1 says Marble decided things would be different this year and announced she was going to race.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "glide" as it is used in Part 1?
options: To jump high into the air
To stop and rest
To move smoothly and slowly
To shout loudly
questions.explanation: Marble begins to glide forward, slow and steady, leaving a silver trail, which shows gliding means moving smoothly and slowly.

questions.questionText: What happens to the rabbits during the race?
options: They nap in the cool shade.
They wander off to nibble clover and forget the race.
They argue about which way to go.
They cross the finish line first.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says the rabbits wandered off to nibble clover and forgot the race entirely. (The grasshopper napped; the beetles argued.)

questions.questionText: What is the main lesson of this story?
options: Slow and steady effort can win in the end.
Friends should always race together.
Snails should never try to race.
It is best to rest when the day gets hot.
questions.explanation: Marble wins by never stopping, showing that slow and steady effort can win, which is the story's lesson.

questions.questionText: Which sentence from the text best shows why Marble was able to win the race?
options: "She did not rush, and she did not quit."
"The day grew hot."
"The other creatures giggled, but Marble paid them no mind."
"Marble the snail lived at the bottom of the garden."
questions.explanation: The sentence that she did not rush and did not quit best explains how her steady effort led to victory.

questions.questionText: How do the other creatures feel about Marble at the END of the story, compared to the beginning?
options: They feel afraid of her the whole time.
At first they laugh at her, but at the end they respect her.
At first they respect her, but at the end they laugh at her.
They ignore her at both the beginning and the end.
questions.explanation: At the start the creatures giggle and laugh at Marble; at the end no one ever laughs at a snail again, showing they now respect her.

questions.questionText: Use both "The Traveler's Soup" and "Marble Enters the Race" to answer this question. What is one way the traveler and Marble are alike?
options: Both succeed by staying calm and not giving up, even when others doubt them.
Both are laughed at by an entire village.
Both win a prize at the end of a contest.
Both use a magic object to reach their goal.
questions.explanation: The traveler stays calm when refused and Marble keeps going when mocked; both succeed by staying steady despite others' doubt.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, where does the salt in the ocean begin?
options: In clouds high in the sky
In rocks and soil on the land
Inside ocean animals
Deep at the bottom of the sea
questions.explanation: Part 1 explains that rainwater picks up bits of salt from rocks and soil on the land and carries them to the ocean.

questions.questionText: Which step happens FIRST in how salt reaches the ocean?
options: Streams flow into rivers.
Rivers carry salt to the ocean.
Water turns into water vapor.
Rain falls on rocks and soil.
questions.explanation: The process begins when rain falls on rocks and soil, picking up salt; then it moves to streams, rivers, and the ocean.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "evaporation" as it is used in Part 2?
options: When water turns into a gas and rises into the air
When rain falls down from the clouds
When rivers flow into the sea
When salt sinks to the bottom of the ocean
questions.explanation: Part 2 says some water turns into a gas called water vapor and rises into the air, and this change is called evaporation.

questions.questionText: According to the text, what happens to the salt when ocean water evaporates?
options: The salt flows back into the rivers.
The salt turns into fresh water.
The salt is left behind in the ocean.
The salt rises into the clouds.
questions.explanation: Part 2 states that when water evaporates, the salt is left behind while the fresh water rises into the sky.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas in Part 1 and Part 2 of the text?
options: Part 1 compares rivers and lakes, and Part 2 compares clouds and rain.
Part 1 explains how salt gets into the ocean, and Part 2 explains why the salt stays and builds up.
Part 1 and Part 2 describe two different oceans on Earth.
Part 2 gives the opposite opinion from Part 1.
questions.explanation: Part 1 tells how salt arrives by way of rivers; Part 2 explains why it stays (evaporation leaves salt behind), so the parts build on each other.

questions.questionText: What is the connection between the sun warming the ocean and the salt staying in the sea?
options: It gives directions in time order only.
It shows cause and effect: the sun causes water to evaporate, which leaves the salt behind.
It lists a problem and then several solutions.
It compares two things that are alike.
questions.explanation: The sun's heat (cause) makes water evaporate, leaving salt behind (effect), so the relationship is cause and effect.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of this text?
options: Rain clouds are made of salt water.
Ocean water tastes bad if you swallow it.
The ocean is salty because rivers bring salt in and evaporation leaves it behind.
The Great Salt Lake is the saltiest place on Earth.
questions.explanation: The whole text explains that rivers add salt and evaporation keeps it in the ocean, which is the main idea.

questions.questionText: Which detail from the text best supports the idea that the ocean keeps getting saltier?
options: "Because new salt keeps arriving and almost none leaves..."
"Have you ever swallowed a mouthful of ocean water..."
"The fresh water rises into the sky, forms clouds..."
"Rain falls on rocks and soil all over the land."
questions.explanation: The detail that new salt keeps arriving while almost none leaves directly explains why the ocean keeps getting saltier.

questions.questionText: Where do giant sequoia trees grow?
options: On the islands of Puerto Rico
In the deserts of Texas
In the forests of California
In the mountains of Nicaragua
questions.explanation: Part 1 states that giant sequoia trees grow deep in the forests of California.

questions.questionText: In Part 1, the author says the sequoia's bark is "like the tree's armor." What does this phrase mean?
options: The bark is made of metal.
The bark protects the tree the way armor protects a knight.
The bark is heavy and slows the tree down.
The bark is bright and shiny.
questions.explanation: Calling the bark like "armor" compares it to protective armor, since the bark protects the tree from insects and fire.

questions.questionText: According to Part 2, how does a forest fire help the sequoia spread its seeds?
options: The fire blows the seeds high into the sky.
Animals carry the burned seeds to new places.
The heat dries the cones so they pop open and drop their seeds.
The fire makes new cones grow on the branches.
questions.explanation: Part 2 explains the fire's heat dries out the cones and causes them to pop open, releasing the seeds inside.

questions.questionText: What is the connection between fire clearing the forest floor and young sequoias growing?
options: It is cause and effect: clearing the floor lets sunlight and nutrients reach the seeds so they can grow.
It lists the trees in order from tallest to shortest.
It presents a problem with no solution.
It compares old trees with young trees.
questions.explanation: Fire clears the floor and adds nutrients (cause), which helps young sequoias grow (effect), a cause-and-effect connection.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the idea about people putting out fires with the idea about the trees today?
options: The author compares California forests with desert plants.
The author lists the steps for planting a sequoia seed.
The author tells two stories that have nothing in common.
The author presents a problem (fewer baby trees grew) and then a solution (rangers set careful fires).
questions.explanation: Stopping fires created a problem (fewer seedlings), and the solution is rangers setting planned fires, so it is a problem-and-solution structure.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of this text?
options: Giant sequoias are huge, strong trees, and fire actually helps them survive.
Forest fires are always dangerous and must be stopped.
Sequoia bark can be used to build houses.
California has the oldest cities in the world.
questions.explanation: The text describes the sequoias' size and strength and explains how fire helps them, which together form the main idea.

questions.questionText: Select the two ways that forest fires help giant sequoias, according to the text.
options: They scare away all the forest animals.
They cause the cones to open and drop seeds.
They clear away dead leaves so sunlight reaches the soil.
They make the trees grow taller in a single day.
questions.explanation: The text says fire opens the cones to release seeds and clears the forest floor so sunlight reaches the soil. The other choices are not stated.

questions.questionText: Where was Roberto Clemente born?
options: Pittsburgh
California
Nicaragua
Puerto Rico
questions.explanation: Part 1 states Roberto Clemente was born in 1934 on the island of Puerto Rico.

questions.questionText: As a boy, what did Roberto use to practice baseball?
options: Equipment given to him by the Pirates
A brand-new bat and glove from a store
Stones and a wooden board
Balls made of old socks and a stick for a bat
questions.explanation: Part 1 says he made his own balls out of old socks and used a stick for a bat.

questions.questionText: Why was life difficult for Roberto when he first joined the Pittsburgh Pirates?
options: He did not want to leave Puerto Rico.
He was not a very good baseball player.
The team would not let him play in games.
He spoke Spanish while most people spoke English, and some were unkind.
questions.explanation: Part 1 explains he spoke Spanish while most people spoke English, and some people were unkind because he came from a different place.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "duty" as it is used in Part 2?
options: A fee that someone has to pay
A type of award given to baseball players
Something a person should do because it is right
A long trip to another country
questions.explanation: The text says Roberto believed talented people had a duty to help others, meaning something they should do because it is right.

questions.questionText: According to Part 2, why did Roberto get on the plane to Nicaragua?
options: To accept an award from the team
To make sure the food and supplies arrived safely
To play in a baseball game there
To visit his family for the holidays
questions.explanation: Part 2 says he got on a plane to make sure the help he gathered for earthquake victims arrived safely.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of this text?
options: Baseball is the most popular sport in Puerto Rico.
Earthquakes are very common in Nicaragua.
Roberto Clemente never faced any problems in his life.
Roberto Clemente was a great baseball player who also worked hard to help others.
questions.explanation: The text describes both his baseball greatness and his work helping others, which together form the main idea.

questions.questionText: Which sentence from the text best supports the idea that Roberto cared about more than baseball?
options: "In 1955, he joined the Pittsburgh Pirates in the United States."
"He visited sick children in hospitals and gave money and food to families in need."
"He won many awards and helped his team win the World Series twice."
"Fans soon noticed his powerful throws and his amazing hits."
questions.explanation: Visiting sick children and giving money and food shows he cared about helping people, not just baseball.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect Roberto's belief about helping others with his actions in Part 2?
options: The author shows that Roberto's belief was the opposite of how he acted.
The author lists Roberto's baseball awards in the order he won them.
The author compares Roberto's beliefs to those of other players.
The author states his belief and then gives examples of him acting on it, such as aiding earthquake victims.
questions.explanation: Part 2 states his belief that talented people must help others, then gives examples (hospitals, donations, the Nicaragua trip) showing he acted on it.

questions.questionText: Use "Giants of the Forest" and "Roberto Clemente: A Hero On and Off the Field" to answer this question. What is one way the two texts are alike?
options: Both describe how people changed their actions after learning something new.
Both tell stories that take place only in Puerto Rico.
Both are poems written with rhyming lines.
Both are mainly about animals that live in the forest.
questions.explanation: In the sequoia text, scientists learned and changed how they handled fire; in the Clemente text, Roberto and others changed actions to help people. Both informational texts show people changing actions after learning, while the other options misdescribe the texts.
